title: The Value of AI-Generated Metadata for UGC Platforms: Evidence from a Large-scale Field Experiment
study_design_description: A large-scale randomized field experiment conducted on a leading short-video platform in Asia from July 20th to August 21st, 2023. Content producers were randomly assigned to either treatment or control conditions. Treatment group producers received access to AI-generated titles on the video posting page and could adjust the titles as needed, whereas control group producers could only write titles by themselves. Randomization effectiveness was verified through pairwise t-tests comparing treatment and control groups on pre-treatment video engagement outcomes, producer characteristics, and video attributes, with no significant differences found.
primary_task_description: Content producers post short videos on Platform A to enhance viewership and engagement and attract new followers, aiming to increase advertising opportunities and revenue. When posting videos, producers are encouraged to add titles with hashtags or descriptions in the title-setting box. Treatment group producers could access an AI-generated title in the title-setting box on the video posting page after they uploaded the video, with a notification indicating that the provided title is generated by AI. Treatment producers had the flexibility to either delete, amend, or fully adopt the AI-generated title.
population_description: The study included 2,048,033 producers who posted at least one video during the treatment period on Platform A, a leading short-video platform in Asia with over 300 million daily active users. During the treatment period, producers in the treatment group uploaded 5,377,560 videos, while those in the control group posted 5,361,424 videos. Additionally, 1,925 treatment group users were surveyed with open-ended questions on the usage of AI-generated titles.

arms.description: Content producers received access to AI-generated titles on the video posting page after uploading the video, with a notification next to the AI-generated title indicating that the provided title is generated by AI. Treatment producers had the flexibility to either delete, amend, or fully adopt the AI-generated title.
Producers could only write titles by themselves without access to an AI-generated title tool.

outcomes.outcome_description: The number of valid watches, designed by the platform to measure the consumption of a video. For videos between 3 and less than 7 seconds, a viewer watch is valid if the watch duration matches the video duration. For videos of 7 seconds or longer, this count is recorded if the watch duration is at least 7 seconds.
Viewers' total watch duration in minutes, calculated as cumulative viewership outcomes for each video over the first two weeks after its posting.
Likelihood of a video having a title.
Area Under the ROC Curve for shares, a metric measuring how well the recommender model predicts viewer engagement behaviors by comparing predicted and actual viewer engagement outcomes.
Area Under the ROC Curve for likes, measuring how well the recommender model predicts viewer engagement behaviors.
Area Under the ROC Curve for follows, measuring how well the recommender model predicts viewer engagement behaviors.
The proportion of content words (such as nouns, verbs, and adjectives) to the total number of words in a text.
The ratio of unique words to the total number of words.
A measure of linguistic diversity in text.
Herfindahl-Hirschman Index measuring platform-level viewership diversity.

The treatment group with access to AI-generated titles had a significantly lower HHI of 0.0002 compared to 0.0003 in the control group, representing a 50% reduction in platform-level HHI.

notes: This is a large-scale randomized field experiment on a leading short-video platform in Asia. The study examines causal effects of AI-generated video titles on content consumption through both intention-to-treat (ITT) and local average treatment effect (LATE) analyses. Key findings include: (1) access to AI-generated titles increased valid watches by 1.6% and watch duration by 0.9% (ITT); (2) adoption of AI-generated titles increased valid watches by 7.1% and watch duration by 4.1% (LATE); (3) the mechanism operates through improved user-video matching accuracy as measured by AUC; (4) AI-generated titles were lower quality than human-generated ones for videos that already had titles (37.9% decrease in valid watches); (5) human-AI co-creation with significant revisions to AI-generated titles outperformed both fully AI-generated and human-only approaches. The study includes mechanism analysis using AUC metrics, heterogeneous effects analysis by content type and producer skill level, lexical richness analysis, viewership diversity analysis using HHI, channel analysis (organic vs. search-oriented recommendations), and multiple robustness checks. Important caveat: Due to technical issues, Platform A only stored AI-generated titles between August 8th and August 21st during the experiment. Additionally, 2,226,922 titled videos (51.56% of total titled videos) in the treatment group did not receive AI-generated titles due to algorithmic issues and were removed from analysis. To protect Platform A's sensitive information, mean values of four key variables (ValidWatch, WatchDuration, Follower, and Following) presented in tables have been scaled by multiplying positive numbers. There is an endogeneity concern that videos with significantly revised AI-generated titles (lower similarity) may inherently indicate higher producer effort and video quality.
